query: complications of APOC2 mutation in children with hypertriglyceridemia

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertriglyceridemia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic causes include syndromes that present primarily with HTG (common) or chylomicronemia (rare). Familial hypertriglyceridemia (excess Very Low-Density Lipoprotein but normal cholesterol) and Familial combined hyperlipidemia (polymorphisms of apolipoprotein C-II (apoC-II), apolipoprotein C-III (apoC-III), etc.) present predominantly with HTG. [6] Lipoprotein lipase deficiency, Apolipoprotein C-II deficiency, Apolipoprotein AV deficiency, and dysbetalipoproteinemia are examples of genetic syndromes that present with chylomicronemia. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Dyslipidemia -- Etiology -- Primary Dyslipidemias
retrieved_docs.content: Familial Chylomicronemia: Familial Chlymicronemia is autosomal recessive and caused by loss of function mutation in lipoprotein lipase (LPL) or LPL gene regulators. Lack of LPL activity leads to reduced or absent hydrolysis of chylomicrons and increased serum TG levels. It is characterized by markedly elevated TG levels (above 1000 mg/dl) and recurrent pancreatitis along with the characteristic lipemic-looking serum.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Dyslipidemia -- Etiology -- Primary Dyslipidemias
retrieved_docs.content: Familial Combined Hyperlipidemia (FCHL): FCHL is the most common genetic dyslipidemia with a prevalence of 1:100. It is present in 40% of myocardial infarction survivors. It is an autosomal dominant condition with variable penetrance. FCHL presents with elevated TG, LDL-C, and apo-B levels and is believed to be from an overproduction of apo-B 100 levels. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Lipemia Retinalis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: However, some authors consider a triglyceride level of 2000 mg/dl as a lower cutoff while defining chylomicronemia and chylomicronemia syndrome. [7] According to these authors (Brunzell and Bierman), features of chylomicronemia syndrome included [7] : Lipemia retinalis in 23% Flushing with alcohol in 25% Documented pancreatitis in 28% Eruptive xanthoma in 40% Objective dyspnea in 46% Abdominal pain and/or pancreatitis in 63% Recent memory loss in 85% of patients Elevation of plasma triglycerides is the main etiology of LR. HTG can be familial or secondary to other diseases. Familial HTG occurs due to: The presence of endogenous circulating lipoprotein lipase ( LPL ) inhibitor Deficiency of LPL Deficiency of apoprotein of LPL i.e., APOC-II. LPL deficiency is an autosomal recessive transmission due to a number of LPL gene mutations like Asp9Asn, Gly188Glu, Pro207Leu, Asp250Asn, Asn291Ser, Ser447X, Pro214Ser, and others. [8] Secondary causes of HTG include [9] :

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Dyslipidemia -- Etiology -- Primary Dyslipidemias
retrieved_docs.content: Familial Hypobetalipoprotenemia (FHBL): Hypobetalipoprotenemia is caused by mutations in the apo-B gene leading to a truncated version of the apolipoprotein B protein. This leads to very low levels of Apo-B (less than 5%) and LDL-C (20-50 mg/dl). In heterozygous FHBL, the apo B levels are at 25% of normal and hepatic steatosis with mild elevation in transaminases can be seen. In homozygous FHBL, which is extremely rare, the presentation is severe with hypercholesterolemia, fatty liver, steatorrhea, and peripheral neuropathy. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Dyslipidemia -- Etiology -- Primary Dyslipidemia
retrieved_docs.content: Familial hypertriglyceridemia is caused by mutations in the LPL gene or the apo C-II gene, which impair the hydrolysis of triglycerides in chylomicrons and very low-density lipoproteins (VLDL), leading to high triglyceride levels and pancreatitis. Familial combined hyperlipidemia is caused by the overproduction of apo B-containing lipoproteins, such as VLDL and LDL, by the liver, leading to high cholesterol and triglyceride levels and insulin resistance. Familial dysbetalipoproteinemia is caused by mutations in the apo E gene, which impair the clearance of chylomicron and VLDL from the blood, leading to high cholesterol and triglyceride levels and xanthomas. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia, Type 2A(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) is an autosomal dominant disorder of lipid metabolism. It is characterized by very high low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) levels due to a gene mutation causing premature coronary heart disease. Homozygous individuals typically acquired disease in childhood. The majority of cases are undiagnosed and sub-optimally treated. Early diagnosis and appropriate treatment are essential in preventing premature death. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Lipoprotein Lipase Deficiency -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most common form of familial chylomicronemia syndrome (FCS) is LPL deficiency, resulting from inactivating mutations in both alleles of the LPL gene. FCS can also result from mutations in other genes that encode proteins essential for LPL activity, including apolipoproteins C-II ( APOC2 ) and A-5 ( APOA5 ), glycosylphosphatidylinositol-anchored high-density lipoprotein (HDL)-binding protein 1 ( GPIHBP1 ), and lipase maturation factor 1 ( LMF1 ). Due to the recessive inheritance pattern, 2 heterozygous parents have a 25% chance of having a child affected by LPL deficiency and a 50% chance of having a heterozygous child with each pregnancy. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Dyslipidemia -- Etiology -- Primary Dyslipidemias
retrieved_docs.content: Abetalipoproteinemia (ABL): Abetalipoproteinemia is caused by mutations in the microsomal triglyceride transfer protein ( MTTP ) gene. The MTTP gene protein is responsible for producing VLDL, LDL, and chylomicrons in the liver and small intestine. Therefore mutation in this gene leads to very low concentrations of TG and cholesterol (under 30 mg/dl) and undetectable levels of LDL-C and Apo-B.

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia, Type 2A(Archived) -- Evaluation -- Untreated LDL-C >500 mg/dL (>13 mmol/L) or treated LDL-C ≥300 mg/dL (>8 mmol/L), AND
retrieved_docs.content: Cutaneous or tendon xanthoma before ten years of age, OR Elevated LDL-C levels consistent with heterozygous FH in both the parents It is important to note, however, that untreated LDL-C levels <500 do not exclude homozygous FH, particularly in young children. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia, Type 2A(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to exclude secondary causes of hypercholesterolemia such as hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome, and liver disease by history and laboratory testing. Diagnosis of FH in most cases is: Clinical , by a personal history of early CAD, family history of premature CAD (definite MI or sudden death, at an age younger than 55 in male first-degree relative or younger than 65 in a first-degree female relative) Physical exam findings (tendon xanthomas and premature corneal arcus) Elevated lipid levels (LDL-C greater than 190 mg/dL)

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hyperchylomicronemia Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: About 80% of familial hyperchylomicronemia syndrome results from inherited defects in both alleles of the lipoprotein lipase gene. The rest, 20%, is the result of a mutation in other genes related to the function of lipoprotein-lipase such as apolipoprotein C-II (APOC2), apolipoprotein A-V (APOA5), high-density lipoprotein binding protein 1 (GP1HBP1), and lipase maturation factor 1 (LMF1). Other unidentified mutations also exist. [1] These all mutations lead to the malfunctioning of the enzyme lipoprotein lipase.

retrieved_docs.title: Lipoprotein Lipase Deficiency -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Familial apoC-II deficiency: Severe chylomicronemia presenting in childhood or adolescence; constitutes 2.0% of monogenic variants. Familial apoAV deficiency: Chylomicronemia presenting in late adulthood; constitutes 0.6% of monogenic variants. Familial LMF1 deficiency: Chylomicronemia presenting in late adulthood; constitutes 0.4% of monogenic variants. Familial GPIHBP1 deficiency: Chylomicronemia in late adulthood; constitutes 2% of monogenic variants. [1] [57] [58] [59] The following secondary causes can also cause hypertriglyceridemia: Diabetes Paraproteinemia and lymphoproliferative disorders Alcohol use Estrogen therapy Medications, including SSRIs, glucocorticoids, atypical antipsychotics, isotretinoin, and certain antihypertensive drugs. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Very Low Density Lipoprotein -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The alteration or deficiency of the enzymes involved in this complex metabolic process may lead to lipid disorders known as hyperlipoproteinemia. These diseases are divided into 5 subtypes depending on the pathophysiology and clinical presentation. Type I hyperlipoproteinemia (familial hyperchylomicronemia) is caused by a deficiency in LPL or Apo C-II, significantly increasing triglyceride concentration. These patients present with eruptive xanthomas, steatosis, recurrent episodes of acute pancreatitis, and no increased risk of atherosclerosis. [11] Defective or deficient LDL receptors or Apo B-100 causes type II hyperlipoproteinemia (familial hypercholesterolemia). This causes an important elevation in LDL (usually >600 mg/dl in homozygotes or >250 mg/dl in heterozygotes) and VLDL cholesterol. These patients present with tuberous xanthomas, xanthelasmas, and premature atherosclerosis, which may lead to acute coronary syndromes in young patients (<20 years of age). [12] Type III hyperlipoproteinemia (familial dysbetalipoproteinemia) is caused by an alteration in Apo-E, which increases VLDL and chylomicrons, leading to premature atherosclerosis. [13] Type IV hyperlipoproteinemia (familial hypertriglyceridemia) is caused by hepatic overproduction of VLDL, leading to a massive increase in triglyceride concentrations (usually >1000 mg/dl), causing premature atherosclerosis and recurrent episodes of acute pancreatitis (similar to type I). [14] [15] Finally, type V (mixed hyperlipidemia) is caused by a sporadic defect in Apo A5 and presents with xanthomas, abdominal pain, and clinical features of hyperglycemia. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypertriglyceridemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Early identification and intervention are crucial, as individuals with FHTG may be asymptomatic until severe complications arise. Recognizing clinical signs such as xanthomas, hepatomegaly, and lipemia retinalis, along with obtaining a thorough family medical history, aids in the timely diagnosis of this condition. Effective management of FHTG requires a multifaceted approach, integrating lifestyle modifications and pharmacological therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Dyslipidemia -- Etiology -- Primary Dyslipidemias
retrieved_docs.content: Homozygous Familial Hypercholesterolemia: Homozygous FH is a rare condition with a prevalence rate of 1 in 1 million. It is caused by biallelic mutations inherited from both parents and is a much more severe presentation than the heterozygous version. LDL levels are often profoundly elevated, usually 4 to 6 times the normal range (above 400mg/dL), and not very responsive to medical therapies. Children will present with xanthomas and xanthelasmas in childhood, by five years of age, and have accelerated coronary atherosclerosis with myocardial infarction in the first decade and death from coronary artery disease in the second decade. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1212
retrieved_docs.content: Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën, a broad classification of metabolic disorders affecting lipoproteins and other lipid abnormalities, includes the specific condition فرط ليبيدات الدم, which denotes hyperlipidemia characterized by elevated blood lipid levels. Recognizing فرط ليبيدات الدم as a child entity within this larger category helps clinicians organize lipid disorders and guide appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic conditions that include hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) as a specific type of lipid disorder. Within this category, elevated blood glucose and new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)) are also considered members, highlighting the clinical overlap between dyslipidemia and early glucose dysregulation. The disorder group is mapped to familial hyperlipidemia type IIa (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ), a well‑characterized lipid abnormality that follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, as indicated by the relationship between Autosomal dominant inheritance and فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ. This interconnected framework underscores how lipid metabolism disorders, specific hyperlipoproteinemia subtypes, and early diabetic manifestations are linked both pathophysiologically and genetically, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1608
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are classified as a form of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and include hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). This category is mapped to type IIa hyperlipidemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ), a condition that follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern (Autosomal dominant inheritance). Recognizing that type IIa hyperlipidemia falls under the broader umbrella of lipid metabolism disorders and is inherited autosomally dominant helps clinicians with diagnosis, genetic counseling, and the management of patients who present with elevated blood lipids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism encompass conditions such as فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia). Within this broader category, فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is a specific manifestation that is mapped to the class of lipid‑metabolism disorders. Type IIa hyperlipidemia follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that Autosomal dominant inheritance is the genetic transmission mechanism for this particular disorder. Recognizing that فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is a form of فرط البروتينات الشحمية and is inherited autosomally dominant helps clinicians diagnose, screen families, and manage patients with lipid metabolism abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1214
retrieved_docs.content: Autosomal dominant inheritance is the mode of transmission for فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (hyperlipidemia type IIa). This genetic pattern means that a single copy of the altered gene inherited from either parent is sufficient to cause the disorder. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is characterized by markedly elevated low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels, which increase the risk of premature atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Because the condition follows an autosomal dominant pattern, it often appears in successive generations, and first‑degree relatives of an affected individual have a 50 % chance of inheriting the same lipid abnormality. Recognizing the autosomal dominant inheritance of فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is essential for family screening, early diagnosis, and implementation of lipid‑lowering therapies to reduce cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 618
retrieved_docs.content: Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën is a subcategory within the broader classification of disorders of lipid metabolism. Within this hierarchy, فرط ليبيدات الدم (hyperlipidemia) is a child of Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën. The general group disorders of lipid metabolism is also linked to فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (hyperlipidemia type IIa), which follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. Clinically, Xanthoma due to primary hypercholesterolemia is mapped to disorders of lipid metabolism, illustrating a typical cutaneous manifestation of these lipid disorders. Understanding these relationships clarifies the classification, genetic basis, and clinical presentation of lipid metabolism disorders, aiding diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 529
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic conditions that encompass several specific entities. Within this category, اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي (metabolic disorder) and فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia) are recognized as subtypes, indicating that these conditions are forms of lipid metabolism disorders. Additionally, ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) (elevated blood glucose/new‑onset diabetes) is listed as a member of disorders of lipid metabolism, highlighting the clinical overlap between dyslipidemia and early diabetes. The condition فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is mapped to disorders of lipid metabolism and is known to follow an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, emphasizing the genetic basis of this specific lipid disorder. Furthermore, اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات (carbohydrate metabolism disorder) is identified as having اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي as its parent, linking carbohydrate metabolic abnormalities to the broader metabolic disorder framework. Together, these relationships illustrate how lipid and carbohydrate metabolic disorders are interrelated, how specific lipid abnormalities can be genetically inherited, and how they may present clinically alongside early diabetic changes, informing a comprehensive approach to patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 679
retrieved_docs.content: Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën (metabolic disorders of lipoproteins and other lipidemias) is classified as a child of the broader category disorders of lipid metabolism. Within disorders of lipid metabolism, فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia) is recognized as a related subclass, and the specific condition فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is mapped to this category. Autosomal dominant inheritance is identified as the inheritance type of فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ, indicating a genetic basis for this form of hyperlipidemia. Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën also has the child concept فرط ليبيدات الدم (hyperlipidemia), further linking various lipid‑related disorders under a common framework. In addition, ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) (hyperglycemia/early diabetes) is listed as a member of disorders of lipid metabolism, illustrating the clinical overlap between glucose dysregulation and lipid abnormalities. This hyperglycemic condition is a member of التجفاف (dehydration) and is associated with تأثير إدرار البول (increased urine output), reflecting its typical physiological effects. Together, these relationships demonstrate that lipid metabolism disorders encompass a spectrum of conditions—including hyperlipoproteinemia, type IIa hyperlipidemia, and broader hyperlipidemia—some of which follow an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, and that they are interrelated with glucose metabolism disturbances such as hyperglycemia, which can lead to dehydration and diuresis. Recognizing these interconnected pathways is essential for comprehensive diagnosis, risk assessment, and integrated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 517
retrieved_docs.content: Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën is a specific subclass of disorders of lipid metabolism, indicating that it falls under the broader category of lipid metabolic disorders. Disorders of lipid metabolism itself is a subcategory of the Arabic‑named category فرص البروتينات الشحمية, placing it within an even wider classification of hyperlipoproteinemia. The disorder group disorders of lipid metabolism is mapped to the condition فرص شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (hyperlipidemia type IIa), which is characterized by an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. Moreover, Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën has a child condition called فرص ليبيدات الدم, further extending the hierarchy of related lipid disorders. This hierarchical organization clarifies how specific lipid metabolism disorders relate to broader hyperlipoproteinemic categories and highlights the genetic inheritance pattern of type IIa hyperlipidemia, aiding clinicians in diagnosis, classification, and genetic counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 435
retrieved_docs.content: "Above reference range" serves as an interpretation used when evaluating "disorders of lipid metabolism." The category "disorders of lipid metabolism" encompasses several more specific entities. It includes the subcategory "Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën" (metabolic disorders of lipoproteins and other lipidemias), which in turn has a child concept "فرط ليبيدات الدم" (hyperlipidemia). Within the broader disorder group, the condition "فرط البروتينات الشحمية" (hyperlipoproteinemia) is recognized as a type of disorder of lipid metabolism, and the specific disorder "فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ" (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is directly mapped to the same overarching category. "فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ" follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, as indicated by the relationship with "Autosomal dominant inheritance." This hierarchical and inheritance information clarifies how various lipid metabolism disorders are related, how they are interpreted in clinical laboratory results, and the genetic considerations important for patient diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2216
retrieved_docs.content: Medium chain triglycerides are contraindicated in patients who have ارتفاع نسبة الدهون الثلاثية في الدم (elevated blood triglyceride levels), indicating that the use of medium chain triglycerides should be avoided in individuals with this condition because it may exacerbate or is not recommended for managing high triglyceride concentrations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 669
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are recognized as a specific form of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and are also classified under hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). Within this category, elevated blood glucose and new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)) are considered member conditions, highlighting the close link between lipid abnormalities and early disturbances in carbohydrate metabolism. Laboratory results that are “above reference range” are interpreted as indicative of a disorder of lipid metabolism, underscoring the diagnostic relevance of lipid panels. The disorder “فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ” (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is mapped to the broader concept of disorders of lipid metabolism and is known to follow an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, indicating that a single copy of the pathogenic allele can transmit the condition. Moreover, carbohydrate metabolism disorders (اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات) are positioned as a parent category to metabolic disorders (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي), reflecting the hierarchical organization of metabolic disease entities. Together, these relationships illustrate how lipid metabolism disorders intersect with broader metabolic pathways, genetic inheritance, and clinical interpretation, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1213

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2049
retrieved_docs.content: The term “vrozené” (congenital) is an allowed qualifier for both “فرط البروتينات الشحمية” and “انفصال الشبكية,” indicating that these conditions can be described as congenital forms. Recognizing the congenital nature of hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) and retinal detachment (انفصال الشبكية) is important for accurate diagnosis, genetic counseling, and tailored patient management.
